segments.text: Human Genome

Major concepts
Much of the genome does not appear to code for anything
The Sequence is easily accessible
The Human Genome sequence is easier to interpret due to extensive homology with model organisms
With cheaper DNA sequencing, we are starting to apply personalized genome analysis to medicine.
C. Venter                                                       F. Collins

From Science
Source of DNA:
2 males, 3 females
African-American
Asian-Chinese
Hispanic-Mexican
2 Caucasians
5.11 Coverage+ Shredded Genbank data 2.9 fold

Genescan
A standard gene-prediction algorithm

Important Characteristics of Human Genome
Size: ~3 billion base pairs
Number of Genes: ~21,000
Unknown Genes ~40%
“Average” gene length ~27,000 bp
Coding-Exons ~1% spread over ~25% of DNA
Repeat content

Genome Composition:
Coding~ 1%
Repeats-at least 50%
Human Gene Example-much of DNA is intron
Categories of Human Genes
Online Mendelian Inheritance in Man (OMIM)
The number of human genes without a mouse homolog, (and vice versa) is less than 1%

Repeats:
Segmental Duplications
Functional Tandem Repeats
Transposon Derived

Repeats:
Segmental Duplications
1077 Blocks of duplicated genes

Blocks of tandemly repeated sequences:
Centromeres- 171 bp in length and is present in tandem 	arrays over a 	megabase of DNA
Telomeres- hexamer in tandem arrays 5-10 kb in length
Ribosomal rRNA gene clusters ~200 genes on 5 different chromosomes
Centromere structure

Telomeres- hexamer in tandem arrays 5-10 kb in length
46 chromosomes, 92 telomeres

Human 47S ribosomal RNA (rRNA) genes comprise the 18S, 5.8S and 28S ribosomal transcripts
Are organized as clusters on acrocentric chromosomes 13, 14, 15, 21 and 22.
As in yeast, the genes are separated by intergenic spacer (IGS) sequences.
Ribosomal RNA genes
Total: 45%

LINEs
Long Interspersed Nuclear Element
6-8 kbp long
Codes for reverse transcriptase/Endonuclease
Non retroviral retrotransposon
Many truncated, nonfunctional insertions
L1 LINES are responsible for germ-line DNA alterations
Why so many Lines?
Of the 850,000 LINEs, only ~50 are actually functional

Sines
Short Interspersed Nuclear Elements
Wildly successful freeloaders
no reading frame
Includes 7SL –Alu element
LINE encoded reverse transcriptase can mobilize SINES

Polymorphisms
Much greater within a group than between, i.e., genetically humans are a single “race.”
SNP’s, Microsatellite, Minisatellite

Minisatellite
DNA element composed of 5-100 bp tandem repeating units of identical sequences. Also called VNTRs.

VNTR’s
Variable Number Tandem Repeats.
Usually as many as 20 codominant alleles
More than 5000 genetic markers of this type have been identified in the human genome
Are used for genetic fingerprinting

Microsatellite
DNA element composed of 15-100 tandem repeats of a 2-5 base pair sequences.
3 Different VNTR Loci in 3 Individuals

SNP’s
Single nucleotide polymorphisms.
Single base pair substitution
Simplest, most prevalent, and most generally useful class of DNA polymorphisms.

2015
~60 mutations not from parents
~3 Million snps between 2 people
Beginnings of Personalized medicine

Characteristics of Human Genome:
Overall size, coding capacity, large scale structure
Repeat organization: Duplications, functional repeats, Transposon derived
Functional repeats: Centromeres, telomeres, rRNA genes
Transposon Derived: Lines, Sines, simple transposons and fossils
Small scale features: Minisatellites (VNTRs) microsatellites, SNPs
Modern Genome sequencing capabilities
LINES induce DNA change in other ways including recombination

Craig Venter                                 Francis Collins
Leaders of Genome Sequencing Teams
